coredata.dc:title: Radiation-synthesis of covalent bonding heterojunctions for selective solar-driven CO2 reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Weidong
Guo, Huazhang
Wang, Kang
Han, Tao
Zhang, Jiye
Wu, Minghong
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The low carrier concentration and sluggish internal charge migration impede the efficiency of CO2 photoreduction in conventional catalysts. Herein, we present an efficient electron beam irradiation strategy to synthesize a carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots (ECN/CQD) heterojunction photocatalyst with an N-bridged covalent interface. This covalent bond at the ECN/CQD interface significantly accelerates the separation and migration of photogenerated charge carriers, resulting in a high concentration of surface charges. As a result, ECN/CQD demonstrates outstanding photocatalytic performance, with CO and CH4 evolution rates of 44.5 and 0.88 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, and excellent stability across eight consecutive cycles. Additionally, in situ Kelvin probe force microscopy and electrostatic force microscopy characterizations reveal the charge distribution on the catalyst surface, providing deep insights into the enhanced charge separation capabilities of the covalent bond heterojunction. This work provides an innovative approach for developing high-performance covalent bond heterojunction photocatalysts for efficient CO2 reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Covalent bond
Heterojunction
CO2 photoreduction
Electron beam irradiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthetic strategy and structural characteristics of catalysts
Optical and photoelectrochemical properties of catalysts
Photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of the catalyst
Preparation of nitrogen-bridged ECN/CQD covalent heterojunction

This work is Supported by Shanghai Technical Service Center of Science and Engineering Computing, Shanghai University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and electronic structure information. a Schematic diagram of the electron beam irradiation-assisted preparation of catalysts. TEM (b) and HRTEM (c) images of CQD. TEM (d) and HRTEM (e) images of ECN/CQD. f FT-IR spectra of CQD and ECQD. High-resolution XPS spectra: O 1s (g) and N 1s (h) of samples. i, j ESR spectra of the samples. k Isosurface (Green and blue regions) of the highest occupied molecular orbital (MO437) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (MO438) of the ECN/CQD model.

In situ KPFM and in situ EFM characterizations. a Schematic diagram of the KPFM measurement. KPFM images of CN in darkness (b) and light illumination (c). KPFM images of CN/CQD in darkness (d) and light illumination (e). KPFM images of ECN/CQD in darkness (f) and light illumination (g). h Corresponding contact potential difference. i Schematic diagram of the EFM measurement, applying bias at the conductive tip and grounding the substrate. j Function diagram of the phase of samples with the applied tip bias. k Normalized function diagram of the phase with the applied tip bias. l Function diagram of the phase of the ECN/CQD with the applied tip bias under dark and light conditions. m The phase images under various tip biases of ECN/CQD in dark and light conditions.

Optical and photoelectrochemical properties of catalysts. a DRS spectra of samples. b Schematic diagram of the energy band structure of ECN/CQD under illumination. c 3D EEM spectrum of ECN/CQD. d In situ EPR spectrum of ECN/CQD. e TRPL spectra of ECN/CQD.

Photocatalytic performance of catalysts and CO2 conversion mechanism. a CO2 photoreduction activity of the synthesized samples and control experiments. The error bars indicate the mean ± s.d. calculated from three independent measurements. b Wavelength dependence of AQYs for ECN/CQD under monochromatic light irradiation. c The time course of the photocatalytic activity of ECN/CQD. d Mass spectrum of the products from the 13CO2 photoreduction by ECN/CQD. e CO2-TPD profiles of CN, ECN, CN/CQD, and ECN/CQD. f Amount of chemically adsorbed CO2 of samples corresponding to CO2-TPD profiles. g, h In situ DRIFTS profiles of ECN/CQD during illumination process. i In situ Raman spectroscopy profiles of ECN/CQD during illumination process. j Possible mechanism pathways of CO2 reduction to CO and CH4.

head.ce:title: Radiation-synthesis of covalent bonding heterojunctions for selective solar-driven CO2 reduction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Organic Compound Pollution Control Engineering (MOE)
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low carrier concentration and sluggish internal charge migration impede the efficiency of CO2 photoreduction in conventional catalysts. Herein, we present an efficient electron beam irradiation strategy to synthesize a carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots (ECN/CQD) heterojunction photocatalyst with an N-bridged covalent interface. This covalent bond at the ECN/CQD interface significantly accelerates the separation and migration of photogenerated charge carriers, resulting in a high concentration of surface charges. As a result, ECN/CQD demonstrates outstanding photocatalytic performance, with CO and CH4 evolution rates of 44.5 and 0.88 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively, and excellent stability across eight consecutive cycles. Additionally, in situ Kelvin probe force microscopy and electrostatic force microscopy characterizations reveal the charge distribution on the catalyst surface, providing deep insights into the enhanced charge separation capabilities of the covalent bond heterojunction. This work provides an innovative approach for developing high-performance covalent bond heterojunction photocatalysts for efficient CO2 reduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into value-added chemicals such as CO and CH4 offers a dual advantage: clean utilization of waste gas and a potential solution to alleviate fossil energy dependence [1–3]. Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) has been widely explored for photocatalysis due to its high stability, activity, and ease of preparation [4,5]. However, its practical use is limited by the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, which leads to low carrier concentrations—crucial for driving photocatalytic reactions [6]. This shortcoming significantly hampers its broader application.

Constructing heterojunctions is a proven strategy to improve charge separation efficiency in photocatalysts [7,8]. A heterojunction can establish an interfacial electric field, driving electrons and holes in opposite directions, thus improving charge separation and prolonging carrier lifetime [9,10]. Carbon quantum dots (CQD), with their sp2-hybridized carbon core and functionalized surface, have attracted widespread attention for their excellent optical and electronic properties, ability to facilitate charge transfer [11]. Therefore, constructing a heterojunction between CQD and CN can broaden light absorption and enhance charge separation.

Most reported heterojunctions rely on weak interfacial interactions such as van der Waals forces [12], π-π attractions [13], and hydrogen bonding [14], which limit charge transfer efficiency. In contrast, covalent bond heterojunctions offer stronger interface bonding, greater stability as well as enhanced charge transfer [15]. For example, Zhao et al. reported a covalent CN-CQD heterojunction formed via freeze-drying and thermal polymerization, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production [16]. In our previous work, we employed an EDC/NHS coupling strategy to construct an amide-linked CQD-CN heterojunction, achieving enhanced CO2 reduction [17]. However, the mechanism by which covalent bond heterojunctions charge migration remains unclear, warranting further investigation.

Electron beam irradiation, a high-energy technique known for polymer cross-linking, can break polymer chains to generate reactive free radicals that facilitate the formation of new chemical bonds [18]. This approach has been applied in the preparation of imine-linked covalent organic frameworks and electron beam curing [19,20]. Here, we utilized electron beam irradiation to construct covalent bond heterojunctions, successfully preparing an ECN/CQD heterojunction with N-bridged covalent bonds at the interface. In situ electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR), Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM), and electrostatic force microscopy (EFM) analyses confirmed that these covalent bonds improve enhance the electronic characteristics of the catalyst surface and enhance efficient charge separation. Furthermore, in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and Raman spectroscopy revealed the presence of *COOH intermediates on the surface of ECN/CQD, which is indicative of effective CO2 reduction. The ECN/CQD heterojunction exhibits excellent photocatalytic CO2 reduction, high CO selectivity, and long-term stability, providing critical insights into the role of covalent interfaces in improving photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic strategy and structural characteristics of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To establish a covalent linkage between CQD and CN, we employed a rapid and efficient electron beam irradiation strategy (Fig. 1 a). Starting with melamine, a rod-like precursor was synthesized via hydrothermal reaction (Fig. S1), which was subsequently annealed to yield tubular CN. Highly crystalline CQD were synthesized using a standard method [21]. When dispersed in water, CQD readily adsorbed onto the tubular CN surface. Under high-energy electron beam irradiation (Fig. S2), bond cleavage and recombination occurred on the surface of both CN and CQD, resulting in the formation of covalent bonds between the two materials.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis (Fig. 1b) revealed uniformly dispersed CQD with an average size of ∼2.5 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging confirmed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1c), corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [22], indicating the high crystallinity of the prepared CQD. The morphologies of ECQD, ECN, and ECN/CQD were consistent with their respective precursors (Fig. 1d and Fig. S3), suggesting that electron beam irradiation did not alter their overall structure. Notably, diffraction fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm were observed on the surface of ECN/CQD (Fig. 1e), evidencing successful covalent integration of CQD with CN. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping (Fig. S4) confirmed that ECN/CQD is primarily comprised of C and N, with trace O. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. S5) and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S6) further validated the preservation of CQD and CN structures post-irradiation [23]. N2 adsorption–desorption analysis (Fig. S7) indicated that the specific surface area of CN increased to 29.7 m2·g−1, roughly 3 times that of bulk CN, suggesting that enhanced binding sites for CQD in the ECN/CQD composite.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Fig. 1f and Fig. S8) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. S9) were used to assess structural changes pre- and post-irradiation. FT-IR spectra for CQD and ECQD showed peaks at 776 and 830 cm−1, corresponding to the deformation vibration of CH in aromatic ring [24,25], and a strong peak at 1567 cm−1 from CC stretching [21]. Broad peaks near 1373 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1 were assigned to OH deformations and stretching modes [21]. The peak at 1270 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching [24]. Post-irradiation, the CO peak in ECQD was significantly intensified, while the CH peak was reduced. The XPS O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) for CQD exhibited three peaks at 531.2 eV, 533.0 eV, and 535.6 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, and adsorbed H2O/O2 [26,27]. The ECQD spectrum showed an enhanced CO peak compared to that of CQD, which is consist with the finding of FT-IR data. These observations confirm that during the irradiation process, chemical bond scission occurs, free radicals are generated, and subsequent recombination leads to the formation of new chemical bonds.

The XPS N 1s spectra (Fig. 1h) of CQD and ECQD indicated residual N, likely from raw materials. The XPS N 1s spectra for CN, ECN, CN/CQD, and ECN/CQD featured peaks at 398.7 eV, 400.2 eV, and 401.3 eV, corresponding to sp2-hybridized N (CNC, N1), sp3-hybridized N (N-(C)3, N2), and amino group (NH, N3), consistent with the typical structure of CN [5,28]. The N1/(N1 + N2 + N3) ratio in ECN/CQD (80.7 %) was higher than in CN (77.0 %), ECN (77.3 %), and CN/CQD (77.5 %), indicating an increase in CNC groups. The ratio of N3/(N1 + N2 + N3) in ECN/CQD (3.9 %) was lower than in CN (7.4 %), ECN (6.8 %), and CN/CQD (7.5 %), indicating a reduction in NH groups. The N2/(N1 + N2 + N3) ratio remained stable across samples. These findings, coupled with the reduction in CH signals post-irradiation in FT-IR, suggest that electron beam irradiation facilitated the cleavage and reformation of NH bonds on CN and CH bonds on CQD, resulting in the generation of CNC linkages, implying covalent bonding via an “N bridge”.

EPR (Fig. 1i, j and Fig. S10) was employed to investigate the electronic characteristics of the samples. All catalysts exhibited a strong EPR signal at g = 2.004, indicative of delocalized electrons [29]. CQD displayed an extremely intense EPR signal, confirming the presence of abundant π-delocalized electrons [17]. These electrons are derived from the internal graphitic structures and the electron-donating hydroxyl groups abundant on the CQD surface [30]. The EPR signal of ECN/CQD was significantly stronger than that of CN, likely due to the high charge density points provided by CQD on the surface of CN, enhancing the concentration of delocalized electrons. Additionally, the signal of ECN/CQD was stronger than that of CN/CQD, attributed to the stable covalent grafting of CQD onto CN by electron beam irradiation, further extending the π-conjugated system. The frontier molecular orbital distribution, calculated via density functional theory (DFT, B3LYP/6-311G) (Fig. 1k and Fig. S11), reveals that the HOMO is predominantly localized at the heterojunction interface and within the CN region, whereas the LUMO resides in the CQD region. This spatial separation suggests enhanced charge separation and rapid charge conduction.

In situ KPFM (Fig. 2 a) was used to assess the photoinduced charge separation capabilities of the catalyst [31,32]. The atomic force microscopy (AFM, Fig. S12) images of CN, CN/CQD, and ECN/CQD exhibited similar morphologies, in line with the TEM images. Under dark conditions, pure CN showed a weak surface potential (Fig. 2b, c), indicating low surface charge density. In comparison, CN/CQD (Fig. 2d, e) showed a significantly higher surface potential, suggesting that CQD adsorbed on the CN surface enhances surface charge. The surface potential of ECN/CQD (Fig. 2f, g) was further increased, indicating that covalent bonding boosts surface charge accumulation. Upon illumination, ECN/CQD showed the largest potential increase (Fig. 2h), confirming the advantage of covalent interfaces in enhancing efficient separation and migration of photogenerated charges.

EFM (Fig. 2i) further probes surface charge characteristics [33,34]. A DC voltage scan ranging from −6 to +6 V applied to the conductive probe induced dipoles in the sample, resulting in negative phase shifts. The EFM phase shift versus voltage was fitted using a second-order polynomial (Fig. 2j). The polynomial fitting results show that ECN/CQD has the largest quadratic coefficient compared to CN and CN/CQD (Fig. S13-15), indicating a higher phase shift, greater dielectric constant and enhanced charge mobility [35]. Additionally, the negative shift of the parabola’s symmetry axis relative to HOPG (Fig. S16) indicated the presence of negative surface charges. Normalized EFM data (Fig. 2k) revealed that ECN/CQD exhibited the largest negative displacement (198 mV), far exceeding CN (8 mV) and CN/CQD (27 mV), suggesting the highest surface charge density [36]. The influence of illumination on the EFM phase shift (Fig. 2l, m and Fig. S17) revealed further phase increases for ECN/CQD, likely due to enhanced light-induced charge separation that synergizes with electric field-induced charges. KPFM and EFM collectively demonstrate that ECN/CQD with a covalent bond interface achieves higher concentration of surface charges and more efficient internal charge transport compared to non-covalent systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photoelectrochemical properties of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Uv–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS, Fig. 3 a) was employed to analyze the light absorption properties of the catalyst [37]. Both CQD and ECQD display broad absorption across the visible spectrum, with absorption edges exceeding beyond 800 nm (Fig. S18), indicating a narrow band gap. In contrast, pure CN and ECN exhibit classic partial visible light absorption. Notably, CN/CQD demonstrates significantly enhanced light absorption in the visible region compared to CN, suggesting that the incorporation of CQD improves the optical absorption performance of CN. The light absorption intensity of ECN/CQD between 450–850 nm is nearly double that of CN/CQD, implying that the electron beam irradiation strategy improves the optical characteristics of the catalyst. The electronic band structures were further elucidated through ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Fig. S19) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy valence band spectrum (VB-XPS, Fig. S20). CN and ECN exhibit similar energy bands, indicating that electron beam irradiation has a relatively weak impact on altering the energy band of CN (Fig. 3b). However, the energy band position of ECQD is significantly shifted upward compared to CQD, endowing ECQD with a more negative energy band position. This implies that the photogenerated electrons on the conduction band of ECQD possess stronger reduction ability [38]. A series of photoelectrochemical tests were conducted to explore charge separation and migration efficiency. Photocurrent measurements (Fig. S21) reveal that ECN/CQD shows the highest photocurrent response, nearly twice that of CN/CQD, proving that the covalent bonding at the heterojunction interface effectively promotes the separation of photogenerated charges [39,40]. Nyquist plots fitted with an equivalent circuit (Fig. S22 and Table S1) demonstrate that ECN/CQD possesses the lowest charge transfer resistance (Rct = 8.9 Ω), indicating that the covalent interface facilitates more efficient charge transport [41].

Excited emission matrix spectra (EEM, Fig. 3c and Fig. S23) were employed to analyze the optical characteristics and recombination of photogenerated carriers. ECN/CQD exhibits lower fluorescence emission than CN, ECN, and CN/CQD, indicating that the heterojunction interface accelerates charge transfer and suppresses carrier recombination [42]. Time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL, Fig. 3d, Fig. S24 and Table S2) further supports these findings, as ECN/CQD exhibits the longest average decay lifetime (τave = 10.71 ns), indicating that the covalent bond interface serves as a high-speed charge transfer channel [43]. This prolongs the residence time of photogenerated carriers in the excited state, increasing the probability of photogenerated charges participating in photocatalytic reactions. In situ EPR spectroscopy (Fig. 3e) was employed to analyze the charge density variation in the catalyst during illumination [44]. As illumination commences, the EPR signal of ECN/CQD gradually intensifies, reaching its peak at approximately 2 min, then remains stable. This behavior indicates efficient photoexcitation-induced electron-hole pair separation within the catalyst, leading to the gradual accumulation of unpaired electrons. Once the maximum concentration is reached, a dynamic equilibrium is established, indicating sustained charge separation during illumination. Overall, these results suggest that the ECN/CQD catalyst exhibits superior photocatalytic activity, attributed to its enhanced light absorption, improved charge separation, and reduced carrier recombination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the catalyst for CO2 reduction was evaluated in a gas–solid reaction system under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). CO and trace amounts of CH4 were detected as the reduction products, while O2 was identified as the oxidation product (Fig. S25). Control experiments conducted no reduction products were detected in the absence of the catalyst, light, or CO2, confirming that CO2 is reduced by the catalyst under illumination (Fig. 4 a). Pure CN, ECN, CQD, and ECQD exhibit low CO production rates of 5.7, 9.1, 1.8, and 1.9 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively. In contrast, the composite CN/CQD significantly enhances photocatalytic activity, achieving an average CO production rate of 36.6 μmol g−1 h−1 and an average CH4 production rate of 0.87 μmol g−1 h−1, nearly six times higher than that of pure CN, and it can maintain catalytic activity within six cycles (Fig. S26). Importantly, ECN/CQD further improves catalytic efficiency, with an average CO production rate of 44.5 μmol g−1 h−1 and CH4 production rate of 0.88 μmol g−1 h−1, outperforming CN/CQD and exceeds most reported catalysts under similar conditions (Fig. S27, Table S3). Additionally, ECN/CQD can effectively catalyze the reduction of CO2 to CO even in low concentration CO2 or air environments (Fig. S28). This highlights the superiority of the covalent bond heterojunction strategy for constructing highly active photocatalysts.

ECN/CQD also exhibits excellent CO selectivity (92.7 %), a relatively high apparent quantum yield (AQY) (0.101 % at 420 nm, Fig. 4b), and robust catalytic stability (Fig. 4c). Isotope-labeled 13CO2 experiment further verifies the source of the product (Fig. 4d), with a distinct the signal for 13CO at m/z = 29, confirming that CO is derived from CO2 rather than the self-decomposition of the catalyst. The CO2 temperature-programmed desorption (CO2-TPD, Fig. 4e) curves analyzed the chemisorption behavior of the catalyst towards CO2. ECN/CQD exhibited a strong CO2 desorption peak at 330 °C, indicating a high density of medium-strength basic sites capable of effectively adsorbing CO2 and promoting desorption of the reduction products [45,46]. Quantitative calculation (Fig. 4f and Fig. S29) confirms that ECN/CQD has the highest chemisorbed CO2 content (128.8 μmol g−1), suggesting that the electron beam irradiation significantly enhances the density of CO2 adsorption sites, contributing to the superior catalytic activity of ECN/CQD.

In situ DRIFTS (Fig. 4g, h) monitored the formation of active intermediates during CO2 photoreduction over ECN/CQD. After moist CO2 gas was introduced into the reaction cell for 20 min to ensure saturation adsorption, infrared signals during the dark reaction revealed the formation of carbonates (Fig. S30) such as b-CO3 2- (1320 cm−1) and HCO3 – (1447 cm−1), indicating effective CO2 activation [47]. Additionally, H2O molecules (3620 and 3727 cm−1) was also detected [48]. When illumination started, new peaks appeared at 1260 and 1590 cm−1, attributed to *COOH, a key intermediate for CO formation [49,50]. Peaks at 1090 cm−1 and 1146 cm−1 were assigned to *CHO and *CH3O, intermediates in CH4 formation [51]. The vibration peak of *OH (3258 cm−1) was also detected [52], while the negative growth of H2O signals confirmed water's involvement in the reaction. In situ Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 4i) further detected signals of *COOH (1237 cm−1), *CO (2860 cm−1), and H2O (1600 cm−1), supporting the formation of key intermediates [53–55]. These observations confirm ECN/CQD’s efficacy in photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Based on the detected intermediates, a possible reduction pathway is speculated (Fig. 4j). CO2 molecules adsorbed on the catalyst surface are reduced to *COOH, which further converts to CO. Some adsorbed *CO is reduced to *CHO and *CH3O, eventually forming CH4.

Band structure analysis indicates that the Fermi level of CQD is higher than that of CN (Fig. S31), driving spontaneous electron transfer from CQD to CN upon contact, which generates an interface electric field pointing from CQD to CN [56]. This field accelerates charge separation, facilitating the recombination of photogenerated electrons in CN’s conduction band (CB) with holes in CQD’s valence band (VB), forming an “S-type” charge transfer mechanism [57]. The relationship between the band structure position of the catalyst with CO2 reduction potentials further supports this mechanism and excludes type II charge transfer. Upon irradiation, photogenerated carriers transfer according to the S-type mechanism, accumulating electrons in the CQD region, efficiently reducing CO2 to CO. In ECN/CQD, the covalent bond at the heterojunction interface acts as a high-speed charge transfer channel, enhancing charge separation and suppressing recombination. Additionally, electron beam irradiation shifts CB of ECQD upwards, boosting the reduction potential of photogenerated electrons. This results in a more potent reduction ability, enhancing the photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of ECN/CQD.

In summary, we successfully constructed an ECN/CQD heterojunction with an N-bridged covalent bond interface using electron beam irradiation, covalently linking narrow-bandgap, hydroxyl-rich CQD to tubular CN. Comprehensive characterization revealed that the covalent bond interface in ECN/CQD significantly improves charge separation efficiency, reduces charge transfer resistance, suppresses fluorescence recombination, and increases surface charge concentration. Moreover, electron beam irradiation enhances active site density and promotes the adsorption and activation of CO2 molecules. The ECN/CQD photocatalyst exhibits high activity, selectivity, and stability in reducing CO2 to CO, achieving an average yield of 44.5 μmol g−1 h−1, which is approximately seven times higher than pure CN. The presence of COOH* species was regarded as a key intermediate in the CO2-to-CO conversion pathway. Mechanism studies reveal that the synergistic effects of the covalent bond interface and the built-in electric field significantly accelerate charge transfer, leading to superior photocatalytic performance. This research not only advances the development of efficient non-metallic photocatalysts but also provides deeper insight into the mechanisms driving their enhanced performance.

Melamine was purchased from Adamas-beta. Pyrene was obtained from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd. NaOH was supplied by GENERAL-REAGENT, and HNO3 by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were used without further purification, and deionized water was employed in the experiment.

CQD were prepared using a previously reported method [21]. In detail, 2 g of pyrene (TCI, purity > 98 %) was refluxed and stirred in hot HNO3 (160 mL) at 80 °C for 12 h to nitrate it into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and diluted with deionized water. The supernatant was removed by standing and this process was repeated several times until the pH of the mixture reached neutral. The residue was dried at 60 °C to obtain yellow 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (3.0 g, 90 % yield). Next, 0.5 g of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was ultrasonically dispersed in NaOH solution (50 mL, 0.2 M) for 30 min, then transferred to a 100 mL high-pressure reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene lining and heated at 200 °C for 10 h to obtain the stock solution. Then, the stock solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane to remove insoluble carbon products. After dialysis (molecular weight cutoff: 3500 Da) to remove sodium salts and unreacted small molecules, the purified black CQD was obtained via freeze-drying.

Tubular CN was prepared via a tandem hydrothermal-calcination strategy. Initially, 4 g of melamine was ultrasonically dispersed in 50 mL of water using ultrasonication for 5 min, followed by transfer to a 100 mL high-pressure reactor. The temperature was raised to 200 °C at 8 °C/min and maintained for 12 h. The reacted mixture was filtered, washed with water, and dried at 60 °C to obtain a white rod-like intermediate. Subsequently, 5 g of the intermediate was placed in a crucible and heated to 550 °C at 2.5 °C/min under flowing N2 atmosphere. After holding at this temperature for 2 h, the sample was naturally cooled to room temperature, yielding tubular CN.

A mixture of 200 mg CN tubes and 10 mg CQD were ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL H2O for 30 min and then stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, the water and unabsorbed CQD were removed by filtration through a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane. Finally, CN/CQD was obtained by freeze-drying.

To prepare electron beam-modified samples, 200 mg of CN (or CQD) powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL H2O for 30 min, and transferred to a transparent ziplock bag. After expelling air, the bag was sealed and subjected to multiple electron beam scans with an absorbed dose of 50 kGy each time, for a total dose of 500 kGy. The energy loss during electron beam penetration was negligible. After freeze-drying, the ECN (or ECQD) samples were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-bridged ECN/CQD covalent heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: To form a covalent heterojunction, 200 mg CN tubes and 10 mg CQD were ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL H2O for 30 min and stirred for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to a ziplock bag. After expelling air, the bag was sealed. The samples were irradiated by electron beam scanning multiple times with an absorbed dose of 50 kGy per instance, for a cumulative dose of 500 kGy. After filtration and freeze-drying, ECN/CQD was obtained.

Weidong Hou: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Kang Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Tao Han: Investigation. Jiye Zhang: Investigation. Minghong Wu: Supervision, Investigation. Liang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21901154), Shanghai Pujiang Program (21PJD022) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2023JJ60522). This work is Supported by Shanghai Technical Service Center of Science and Engineering Computing, Shanghai University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mattod.2025.01.016.

ce:section.ce:para:
